---
title: "*snakeATAC.utility* overview"
date: "Last update: `r format(Sys.time(), '%d<sup>th</sup> %B, %Y')`"
author:
- name: "Sebastian Gregoricchio, Ph.D."
  affiliation:
    - "Netherlands Cancer Institute, Oncogenomics division, Amsterdam - Netherlands"
  email: 's.gregoricchio@nki.nl'
package: "*[snakeATAC.utility](https://github.com/sebastian-gregoricchio/snakeATAC/tree/main/utility/snakeATAC.utility)*"
output:
  prettydoc::html_pretty:
    highlight: github
    math: katex
    theme: cayman
    number_sections: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 4
    fig_caption: true
    fontsize: 11pt
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{snakeATAC.utility overview}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

<style>
body {
text-align: justify}
</style>


```{r setup, include = FALSE}
# knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = ">", dev = "svg",
#                       warning = F, message = F, fig.align = "center",
#                       rows.print=12)
#knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = rprojroot::find_rstudio_root_

if (Sys.info()[["sysname"]] == "Darwin") {
  knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = ">",
                        warning = F, message = F, fig.align = "center",
                        rows.print = 12, dev = "png", dpi = 96)
} else {
  knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = ">",
                        warning = F, message = F, fig.align = "center",
                        rows.print = 12, dev = "png", dpi = 96)
}

#options(tibble.print_min = 4L, tibble.print_max = 4L)


# Load libraries required
require(snakeATAC.utility)
```

![license](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-GPLv3-blue.png)

----------------------------------------------------------

# **Introduction** {#intro}
The R-package *snakeATAC.utility* is a companion R utilities for the `snakeATAC` pipeline, a Snakemake workflow for the analysis of ATAC-seq data. Provides functions to import, re-analyse and re-plot pipeline outputs — including sample-level quality control (principal component analysis and correlation heatmaps from genome-wide coverage), transcription-factor footprints and differential-binding volcanoes from TOBIAS results, filtering of HOCOMOCO motif collections, and summaries of pipeline runtime and resource usage — so that results can be inspected and figures customized directly in R.

<br>


## Citation
If you use this package, please cite:

<div class="warning" style='padding:2.5%; background-color:#c1eac4; color:#226a27; margin-left:5%; margin-right:5%; border-radius:15px;'>
<span>
<font size="-0.5">
<p style='margin-top:1em; text-align:left'> **Citation** </p>

<div style="margin-left:2%; margin-right:2%; text-align: justify">
<i>snakeATAC.utility</i>: ...<br>
Gregoricchio S.<br>
<i>XYZ</i> (20XY)<br>
doi: <a href="https://doi.org/XYZ/XYZ">XYZ</a>
</div>
</font>

</span>
</div>

<br>

```{r citation, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
citation("snakeATAC.utility")
```

<br>

----------------------------------------------------------

# **Filter `BINDetect` results** {#filterbindetect}
The `snakeATAC` differential TF-binding step runs `TOBIAS BINDetect` on a pair of group-level footprint tracks and writes, per comparison, a `bindetect_results.txt` table plus one sub-folder per transcription factor (TF).<br>
A common follow-up question is: *within a specific set of regions* (a promoter set, a set of differential peaks, a ChIP-seq target list, ...), which TFs are differentially bound between the two conditions?

The function `subset_BINDetect` from `snakeATAC.utility`:
- Overlaps the existing per-site output with your BED and re-summarises
- Normalization kept genome-wide (as computed on the full peak set)
- Volcano centering: inherited from the full run
- Interprets the effect relative to a genome-wide background

>**NOTE**: In BINDetect the score normalization between
conditions, the bound/unbound threshold, and the background model for the
p-values are all estimated from the **whole** peak set. If your regions are
biased (e.g. generally more accessible in one condition), rebuilding the
background from that subset alone skews the distribution and de-centres the
volcano. The function avoids this by keeping the background genome-wide.

<br>

## Inputs from a `snakeATAC` run
The function starts from the differential TF-binding outputs produced by the `snakeATAC`(/`TOBIAS`) pipeline (folder `05b_Differential_TF_binding_TOBIAS`).

`subset_BINDetect()` reads the per-TF `*_overview.txt` files, keeps the binding sites overlapping `roi_bed` (regions of interest bed), and recomputes per-TF summaries. The per-site log2 fold changes are used exactly as BINDetect computed them (already normalized between conditions); by default each TF is tested against a **genome-wide** background of per-site log2 fold changes.

```{r, eval=FALSE}
res_subset <-
  subset_BINDetect(bindetect_dir = bindetect_dir, # path
                   bed = roi_bed, # GRanges, file_path or data.frame/data.table
                   condition1 = "groupA",
                   condition2 = "groupB",
                   background = "all", # genome-wide null (default)
                   summary_stat = "mean",
                   bound_in_any = FALSE,
                   min_sites = 10,
                   top_quantile = 0.05)

head(res_subset) # one row per TF, sorted by padj then |subset_change|
sum(res_subset$top) # TFs in the volcano tails
```

The `subset_change` column is the mean (or median) of the subset per-site log2 fold changes; negative values mean higher binding in `condition2`. It is deliberately **not** named `change`, because it is a re-derived quantity — a Wilcoxon test of the subset against the background — and is not numerically identical to BINDetect's own change/p-value machinery. If you need numbers
directly comparable to a real run, re run TOBIAS manually using your subset-regions as `--peaks`.

<br>

## Choosing the background
The `background` argument decides *which question you answer*:

* `background = "all"` (default): the null is every binding site genome-wide. Question: **within my regions, which TFs are differentially bound between the two conditions?** This matches the "true background = full peak set" logic.
* `background = "outside"`: the null is, per TF, that TF's sites **not** overlapping `roi_bed`. Question: **for which TFs is the differential binding specific to my regions** relative to the rest of the genome?

```{r background-outside, eval=FALSE}
res_specific <- subset_bindetect_by_bed(
  bindetect_dir = bindetect_dir,
  bed = roi_bed,
  condition1 = "groupA",
  condition2 = "groupB",
  background = "outside")
```

<br>

## The `bound_in_any` filter
Setting `bound_in_any = TRUE` keeps only sites predicted bound in at least one condition before summarising. This is a reasonable way to drop empty sites, but note it **biases the log2fc distribution**: it removes unbound–unbound sites, which cluster near zero, and therefore shifts the top-quantile selection. It is exposed as an option rather than hard-coded; compare both if in doubt.

```{r bound-filter, eval=FALSE}
res_bound <-
  subset_BINDetect(bindetect_dir = bindetect_dir,
                   bed = roi_bed,
                   condition1 = "groupA",
                   condition2 = "groupB",
                   bound_in_any = TRUE)
```

<br>

## Selecting top TFs
The `top` column mirrors the BINDetect volcano highlight: a TF is flagged when its `subset_change` falls in the lower/upper `top_quantile` tails and/or its `-log10(padj)` exceeds the upper tail. Remember that BINDetect works on **motifs/TFs, not genes** — with a few hundred motifs, "top 5%" is only a handful of TFs. To rank *sites* instead, work from the per-site `log2fc` column in the `*_overview.txt` files directly.

```{r top, eval=FALSE}
top_TFs <- res_subset[res_subset$top, c("TF", "subset_change", "padj")]
top_TFs
```

<br>

----------------------------------------------------------

# **Filter HOCOMOCO motifs** {#filterhoco}
`filter.hocomoco.jaspar()` reads a HOCOMOCO motif collection in JASPAR format and
returns the subset you care about -- selected by gene symbol, by motif ID, and/or
by motif quality -- as a list of `universalmotif` objects, optionally written back
out in JASPAR format.

Selection by gene is bridged through the HOCOMOCO *annotation* file rather than by
parsing the motif ID, because a motif-ID prefix is a UniProt entry-name mnemonic
and not always the HGNC gene symbol (`ANDR` &rarr; *AR*, `AP2A` &rarr; *TFAP2A*,
`SRBP2` &rarr; *SREBF2*). Matching the raw prefix would silently drop those factors.

<br>

## Getting the HOCOMOCO files
Two files are used, both available from the **Downloads** page at <https://hocomoco.autosome.org> (this generic link always resolves to the current release):

- the motif collection in **JASPAR format** (e.g. `H14CORE_jaspar_format.txt`), which is the input to filter;
- the **annotation** file (e.g. `H14CORE_annotation.jsonl`), needed only when filtering by gene or renaming motifs to gene symbols.

```{r}
jaspar_file <- "H14CORE_jaspar_format.txt"
annotation_file <- "H14CORE_annotation.jsonl"
```

The example chunks below are not evaluated, since they require these downloaded
files.

<br>

## Filtering
```{r, eval=F}
# By gene symbol (case-insensitive; a motif is kept if any of its genes matches):
motifs <- filter.hocomoco.jaspar(jaspar_file = jaspar_file,
                                 genes = c("CTCF", "GATA1", "AR"),
                                 annotation_file = annotation_file)
```

Motif IDs encode the model **subtype**, **experiment** type, and **quality** grade
-- e.g. `CTCF.H14CORE.0.PSM.A` is subtype 0, evidence P+S+M, quality A. To keep only
the primary, high-quality models, use the `subtypes` and `qualities` arguments; the
familiar "0.A / 0.B" shorthand corresponds to `subtypes = 0, qualities = c("A", "B")`
(in v14 the experiment letters sit between the subtype and quality fields).

```{r, eval=F}
best <- filter.hocomoco.jaspar(jaspar_file = jaspar_file,
                               genes = c("CTCF", "GATA1", "AR"),
                               subtypes = 0,
                               qualities = c("A", "B"),
                               annotation_file = annotation_file)
```


Or pick specific motifs by their exact IDs (no annotation needed unless you also
rename to gene symbols):

```{r, eval=F}
picked <- filter.hocomoco.jaspar(jaspar_file = jaspar_file,
                                 motif_ids = c("CTCF.H14CORE.0.PSM.A", "GATA1.H14CORE.0.PSM.A"))
```

<br>

## Naming and export
Choose whether the returned motifs are named by motif ID (the default) or by gene
symbol; the motif ID is always retained in the `altname` slot. Supplying `out_file`
writes the result with `universalmotif::write_jaspar()`:

```{r, eval=F}
filter.hocomoco.jaspar(jaspar_file = jaspar_file,
                       genes = "CTCF",
                       annotation_file = annotation_file,
                       names_as = "gene",
                       out_file = "ctcf_motifs.jaspar")
```

With no `out_file`, the filtered motifs are simply returned to the console for
interactive use or downstream scanning (for example `universalmotif::scan_sequences()`,
or conversion to a `PWMatrixList` for use with **motifmatchr**).

<br>

------------------------------------------------------

# **Sample correlation heatmap** {#corrheatmap}
snakeATAC computes pairwise sample correlations with deepTools `plotCorrelation` and writes the coefficient matrices alongside its own heatmaps.<br>
`correlation.heatmap()` turns one of those matrices into a clustered heatmap (`ggplot2`) with a dendrogram, and can optionally return the underlying `hclust` and distance objects.

<br>

## Input
A snakeATAC correlation matrix (`.txt`), Spearman or Pearson:

```
06_Overall_quality_and_info/Sample_comparisons/Sample_correlation/Correlation_matrix_on_BigWigs_wholeGenome_spearmanMethod.txt
06_Overall_quality_and_info/Sample_comparisons/Sample_correlation/Correlation_matrix_on_BigWigs_wholeGenome_pearsonMethod.txt
```

A path (read with `data.table::fread`) or an in-memory `data.frame` are both accepted.

<br>

## Usage
```{r, eval=F}
corr <- file.path("06_Overall_quality_and_info", "Sample_comparisons",
                  "Sample_correlation",
                  "Correlation_matrix_on_BigWigs_wholeGenome_spearmanMethod.txt")

correlation.heatmap(corr)
```

```{r, eval=F}
# Tighten the colour range, move the dendrogram, use Ward clustering, hide labels:
correlation.heatmap(correlation.matrix = corr,
                    correlation.scale.limits = c(0.8, 1),
                    dendrogram.position = "top",
                    clustering.method = "ward.D2",
                    display.values = FALSE)

# Drop samples and also return the clustering objects:
out <- correlation.heatmap(correlation.matrix = corr,
                           excluded.samples = c("sampleX", "sampleY"),
                           return.cluster.object = TRUE)
out$heatmap
out$cluster
```

The palette accepts any color vector (default: reversed viridis *mako*); set `exclude.diagonal = TRUE` to blank the self-correlations.

<br>

------------------------------------------------------------

# **PCA plot** {#pcaplot}
To compare samples, snakeATAC computes genome-wide coverage over fixed bins with deepTools `multiBigwigSummary` and already emits a PCA under `06_Overall_quality_and_info/`. `read.multibigwigsummary()` re-imports that matrix into R and `plot.PCA()` regenerates a fully customisable PCA as a `ggplot2` object.

<br>

## Input
The `multiBigwigSummary` output (`.npz`), written by snakeATAC to:

```
06_Overall_quality_and_info/Sample_comparisons/Sample_correlation/multiBigWigSummary_matrix_allSamples.npz
```

(The same functions also work on the peak-score matrix
`06_Overall_quality_and_info/Sample_comparisons/Peak_comparison/peaks_score_matrix_all_samples_<peakcaller>.npz`.)

`read.multibigwigsummary()` reads the `.npz` through **reticulate**, so an R-visible Python with **numpy** installed is required.

<br>

## Usage
```{r, eval=F}
npz <- file.path("06_Overall_quality_and_info", "Sample_comparisons",
                 "Sample_correlation", "multiBigWigSummary_matrix_allSamples.npz")

mat <- read.multibigwigsummary(npz)   # columns = samples, rows = genomic bins
dim(mat)
colnames(mat)
```

```{r, eval=F}
# Default PCA on PC1 / PC2:
plot.PCA(mat)

# Drop samples, look at PC2 vs PC3, and set custom (named) colours:
plot.PCA(matrix = mat,
         excluded.samples = "sampleX",
         PCs = c(2, 3),
         color.list = c(WT_rep1 = "#1b9e77", WT_rep2 = "#1b9e77",
                        KO_rep1 = "#d95f02", KO_rep2 = "#d95f02"),
         point.size = 4)
```

`scale` and `center` (both `TRUE` by default) are passed to `prcomp()`. The return value is a `ggplot2` object, so it can be themed further or saved with `ggsave()`.


------------------------------------------------------

# **Benchmark summary** {#benchmark}
Snakemake writes one benchmark file per rule into a `benchmarks/` folder in the snakeATAC output directory.<br> `benchmark.summary()` aggregates them into a per-rule table of total runtime and peak resources, with a final `SUMMARY` row.

```{r, eval=F}
bench <- benchmark.summary("path/to/snakeATAC_output/benchmarks")
bench
```

The returned `data.frame` has one row per pipeline rule -- number of steps, total running time (in minutes and as `dd.hh.mm.ss`), maximum physical and virtual memory (GB) and the average CPU load -- followed by a `SUMMARY` row totalling the run.



<br>

-----------------
# **Package information** {#info}
## Documentation
With the package a detailed [web-manual](https://sebastian-gregoricchio.github.io/snakeATAC/utility/snakeATAC.utility/manual/index.html) with details for each function and respective parameters is available.

The R-package has been published on [GitHub](https://github.com/sebastian-gregoricchio/snakeATAC/tree/main/utility/snakeATAC.utility). At these site it is possible to find the installation procedure, required dependencies, and the links for changeLog, manual and vignette.

<br>

## Package history and releases
A list of all releases and respective description of changes applied could be found [here](https://sebastian-gregoricchio.github.io/snakeATAC/utility/snakeATAC.utility/NEWS).


<br>

## Contact
For any suggestion, bug fixing, commentary please contact fill an [Issues](https://github.com/sebastian-gregoricchio/snakeATAC/issues)/[Pull requests](https://github.com/sebastian-gregoricchio/snakeATAC/pulls) form on the `snakeATAC` github page.

![](https://badges.pufler.dev/contributors/sebastian-gregoricchio/snakeATAC?size=50&padding=5&bots=true)


## License
This package is under a GNU General Public License (version 3).
